perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it shall protect the confidentiality of, and take reasonable steps to prevent disclosure or unauthorized use of, the Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons not legally bound to maintain its confid entiality, provided that in no event shall such party's obligations exceed the reasonable standard of care taken to protect its own confidential information of like importance.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to take steps to protect the confidentiality of, and prevent disclosure or unauthorized use of, the Confidential Information. The level of protection required will be determined by what is deemed appropriate based on the current business climate.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'take reasonable steps' with 'take steps' and introducing the vague standard of 'what is deemed appropriate based on the current business climate' introduces ambiguity. This makes the obligation unclear and potentially unenforceable, weakening the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: ARS § 44-7051 defines misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the required level of protection for confidential information ambiguous, the modified text makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation under Arizona law, as the 'reasonable steps' requirement is replaced with a vague and subjective assessment of 'what is deemed appropriate'.

The official version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is published by Thomson Reuters. ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 44 Next Section Printable Version 44-7051 - Consumer protection 44-7051. Consumer protection A. Nothing in this chapter diminishes the parties' consumer protection rights prescribed in chapter 10, article 7 of this title or any other federal or state law relating to consumers. B. If a consumer law, other than this chapter, requires a paper record or notice of the transaction, the parties to the transaction may request that the record or notice be provided in an electronic format and that record or notice shall comply with this chapter. Even if before completing a consumer transaction by an electronic method that complies with this chapter, a party to the transaction requests that a record or notice of the transaction be delivered in electronic form, that party may subsequently change that preference and request that all future records or notices relating to that transaction be sent in paper form to an appropriate address. Withdrawal of consent does not affect the enforceability of electronic records or notices previously provided or made available to that party in accordance with this chapter. C. A nonelectronic consumer contract or agreement may not contain a provision that authorizes any transaction or part of any transaction pursuant to that contract or agreement by electronic means unless all of the following apply: 1. The consumer makes a separate and express assent or signing either manually or electronically that specifies that the consumer agrees that certain transactions or parts of transactions will be conducted by electronic means. 2. The contract or agreement indicates which transactions or parts of transactions that may be conducted by electronic means and the manner in which those transactions or parts of transactions shall be conducted. 3. The consumer agrees, as part of the assent, to provide the other party with the consumer's electronic address that complies with section 44-7015. 4. The consumer agrees, as part of the assent, to notify the other party, either manually or electronically, o
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language replaces the 'reasonable steps' standard with a subjective assessment of 'what is deemed appropriate based on the current business climate,' which introduces ambiguity and weakens the protection of confidential information. This ambiguity makes it difficult to prove misappropriation under Arizona law. The snippet provided focuses on consumer protection rights and electronic transactions and does not directly discuss trade secrets or the standard of care required to protect confidential information. However, the Law Explanation explicitly states that this modification makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation under Arizona law, due to the vague and subjective nature of the new standard. This weakening of protection and shift to a subjective standard creates a contradiction by making enforcement against misappropriation less certain.

perturbation.original_text: Any materials, documents, notes, memoranda, software code, drawings, sketches and other tangible items containing, consisting of or relating to the Confidential Information of a party which are furnished hereunder to the other party, or are in the possession of the other party, remain the property of the party which disclosed the Confidential Information and shall be promptly returned to such party upon the disclosing party’s request therefore.
perturbation.changed_text: Materials, documents, notes, and other items relating to the Confidential Information should be returned to the disclosing party upon request, subject to reasonable business needs.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that all tangible items 'shall be promptly returned' upon request. The modified text changes this to 'should be returned...subject to reasonable business needs.' The added clause introduces ambiguity, allowing the receiving party to potentially delay or refuse the return based on their interpretation of 'reasonable business needs.'
perturbation.law_explanation: This weakens the obligation to return confidential information and introduces a basis for dispute over what constitutes a 'reasonable business need'. It could affect a trade secret action under ARS § 44-7051 by making it more difficult to regain possession of misappropriated trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by adding 'subject to reasonable business needs,' which weakens the original obligation to promptly return confidential information. This directly undermines the protection of trade secrets, as the receiving party can now potentially delay or refuse the return based on their interpretation of 'reasonable business needs.' While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on consumer protection under ARS § 44-7051, the Law Explanation suggests this weakening could affect trade secret actions under the same statute by making it more difficult to regain possession of misappropriated trade secrets. Although the snippet doesn't directly address trade secrets, the introduction of ambiguity creates a loophole, enabling potential non-compliance with the goal of protecting confidential information, thus contradicting the intent of ARS § 44-7051 as interpreted by the LLM.
